title: Comb honey using Ross rounds
subtitle: Getting round sections of cut comb from a busy summer colony.
excerpt: Ross rounds are plastic circular cassettes that fit inside a standard super and let bees build and cap comb sections the consumer can eat whole. They work in a strong summer colony on a good flow and give a premium product with almost no processing after harvest.
sourceNotes: BBKA comb honey guidance, bbka.org.uk. Morse, R. A., Comb Honey Production (1978), widely cited technical reference. National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com.

glossaryTerms.definition: A circular plastic cassette that clips into a modified super frame. Bees build comb and cap it inside the cassette, producing a round section of honeycomb sold whole.

A period when one or more plants are producing enough nectar for bees to collect and process into honey in quantity. Major UK summer flows: oil seed rape (May), clover and lime (June to July), heather (August).
The thin wax layer bees use to seal a full honey cell. White flat cappings are the sign of ripe honey in a comb section.

content.text: Comb honey sells at a premium because it is honey in its most unprocessed form.
Ross rounds

reduce the difficulty of cut-comb production: the plastic cassettes produce a uniform product and clip shut for display without any cutting. The limitation is that they demand a strong colony on a strong
nectar flow
. A weak flow produces partial sections that the bees won't finish.
When to use them

Ross rounds work best during the main summer flow, typically late June through July in most of England. The colony should be filling a standard super within ten days. Putting rounds on a colony mid-flow or late in a poor season results in uncapped sections that cannot be sold.

The colony must be strong: at least ten frames of bees in the brood box and an existing super already partly filled. Load the rounds super directly above a full or nearly full first super, not above the queen excluder on an otherwise empty hive.
Setting up the rounds

Load each cassette with a thin starter strip of pure beeswax (available from beekeeping suppliers) rather than foundation. The starter guides the bees to build straight comb within the cassette. Clip the cassettes into the modified frames; a standard Ross round super holds about twenty sections.
Smoke the hive lightly and use the
honey super
procedure to place the rounds super above the existing super. Do not place it between the queen excluder and the first super.
Harvesting

Use a clearer board the evening before harvest to clear the bees from the rounds super. Twenty-four hours later the super should be bee-free. Remove it and work through each frame: cassettes with fully capped
cappings
across the whole face are ready. Partially capped sections can go back on the hive or be cut out and eaten fresh, they won't store.

Clip the cassette closed, wipe the outside with a barely damp cloth, and place in the lid. Sections stored at room temperature will stay fresh for six months to a year. Do not refrigerate, cold crystallises the honey and the texture changes.

Sections produced on clover or lime have a pale, clean flavour. Sections from oilseed rape granulate inside the cell within weeks of capping and become hard; they're not suitable for rounds. Know your local flow before loading rounds in early May.
Cleaning and storing the cassettes

After harvest, empty cassettes can be scraped clean with a warm hive tool and reloaded with a fresh starter strip for the following season. Propolis and burr comb come off more easily when the cassettes are slightly warm. Avoid washing in water, residual moisture in the cassette encourages mould.
